title: SEGMENT 9 — Phase 3 endpoints — surrogate, clinical, and the Casgevy negotiation

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Phase 3 endpoints — surrogate, clinical, and the Casgevy negotiation

If a gene therapy is going to be tested in a traditional Phase 3 trial — a larger, often randomized, often controlled study designed to definitively establish efficacy — one of the most important conversations between sponsor and agency is about endpoints.
What are you going to measure that will determine whether the drug works?
Endpoints come in two broad categories.
Clinical endpoints are direct measures of how the patient feels, functions, or survives.
Death.
Hospitalization.
Pain.
Mobility.
Cognition.
These are the things that matter directly to patients and their families.

They are also, often, very hard to measure efficiently — because some of them happen rarely, some of them take years to manifest, and some of them require enormous sample sizes to detect a difference between treatment and placebo.
Surrogate endpoints are biomarkers or other measurements that are believed to predict clinical benefit but are not themselves clinical benefit.
Blood pressure for cardiovascular disease, viral load for HIV, LDL cholesterol for cardiovascular risk, hemoglobin A1C for diabetes complications — these are all surrogates.
They are easier to measure than the underlying clinical outcome, and if they have been validated as predictors of clinical benefit, the agency may accept changes in them as the basis for approval.
For gene therapy, surrogate endpoints are particularly attractive.
Many gene therapies produce dramatic, measurable changes in a biomarker — say, levels of a missing enzyme, or numbers of edited cells — long before any clinical endpoint becomes measurable.
If the surrogate has been validated, the trial can be smaller, faster, and feasible in a population where running it to a hard clinical endpoint would be impractical.
The challenge is that for any given disease, the validity of a particular surrogate is debatable.
Just because a biomarker correlates with disease does not mean that changing the biomarker will change the disease.
The history of medicine is full of cautionary tales — drugs that lowered the surrogate but didn't help patients, and sometimes hurt them.
Antiarrhythmic drugs that suppressed PVCs but increased mortality.
Drugs that raised HDL cholesterol but didn't reduce cardiovascular events.
The agency, having learned these lessons, is properly cautious about accepting new surrogate endpoints.
The negotiation between sponsor and agency over what endpoint a pivotal trial will use is, often, one of the most consequential conversations in a gene therapy program.
And the example I want to tell you about, because it shaped how the field thinks about this kind of negotiation, is Casgevy for sickle cell disease.
Sickle cell disease is, you know, an inherited disorder of hemoglobin.

Patients have a single amino acid change in beta-globin that causes red blood cells to sickle under low oxygen, leading to episodes of severe pain called vaso-occlusive crises, organ damage over time, and shortened life expectancy.

Casgevy — the CRISPR therapy approved in December 2023 — uses gene editing to disrupt a regulator called BCL11A in patients' bone marrow stem cells, reactivating fetal hemoglobin production and bypassing the sickling problem.
The endpoint question for the Casgevy trial was: what should you measure to demonstrate that the therapy works?
Mortality would be the cleanest clinical endpoint, but mortality from sickle cell takes decades to manifest, and you could not run a trial that long.
Pain itself is hard to standardize.
What the sponsors and the agency eventually converged on was the elimination of vaso-occlusive crises over a defined follow-up period.
Patients in the trial were assessed for their history of crises before treatment, treated with Casgevy, and then followed for at least twelve consecutive months after engraftment.
The pre-specified endpoint was the proportion of patients who experienced no vaso-occlusive crises during that twelve-month window.
This was, in a sense, an intermediate endpoint — neither a pure surrogate like a blood biomarker, nor a hard mortality endpoint, but a clinically meaningful event count over a defined period.

The agency accepted this design because vaso-occlusive crises are themselves the proximate cause of much of the disease's morbidity, are reliably countable, and represent something patients and clinicians both care about deeply.

The trial data — published in the New England Journal of Medicine, presented at advisory committees — showed that the vast majority of treated patients were free of crises throughout the follow-up window.
That was the basis for approval.
The Casgevy endpoint negotiation is a template now.
Future CRISPR and gene therapy programs in other diseases are looking at this case and asking: what is the equivalent endpoint for our condition?
What is the clinically meaningful event we can count over a defined window that the agency will accept as the basis for approval?
This is, in a real sense, a regulatory creativity problem as much as a scientific one.
The answer for sickle cell was vaso-occlusive crises.
The answer for a different disease will be something different.
But the framework — choose an intermediate clinical endpoint that is countable, meaningful, and measurable within a feasible trial timeline — is the model.
